content.full_text:
,1P
R’vi MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR PoLITIcAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
March26, 1997
Honorable Chris P. Mathys
Councilmember, City of Fresno
2051 E. Shaw Avenue
Fresno, California 93710
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-062
Dear Mr. Mathys:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding your duties under the
provisions of Proposition 208 and the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTIONS
1) During the off-year ban on accepting campaign contributions, may you, as a
councilmember for the City of Fresno, solicit contributions for the purpose of opening a district
office? If so, what is the maximum you can raise and what are the requirements of maintaining a
district office? May the district office be located inside your home?
2) How may campaign funds raised prior to January 1, 1997, be used?
3) If you are permitted to raise money for the district office, what are the maximum
amounts that can be contributed by individuals, companies, small contributor committees, and
political action committees?
CONCLUSIONS
1) You may raise up to $10,000 per calendar year to establish and maintain an
officeholder account. You may use officeholder account funds to open a district office as long as
you do not use the district office in connection with any campaign for elective office or ballot
measure. The district office may be located inside your home.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-062
Page 2
2) You may use campaign funds raised prior to January 1, 1997, consistent with
regulations 18530.1 and 18519.4.
3) The maximum amount of a contribution to an officeholder account must not exceed
$250 per calendar year from any person.
ANALYSIS
Officeholder Account
During the off-year ban, you are prohibited from accepting campaign contributions.
(Section 85305.) However, as an elected officer, you may establish an officeholder account
pursuant to section 853 13(a). You may also raise money to establish and maintain this
officeholder account. Under section 853 13(a), aggregate contributions to your officeholder
account must not exceed $10,000 per calendar year. Therefore, once you raise $10,000 into your
officeholder account, you may not raise additional funds into the account until the next calendar
year. (Weldy Advice Letter, No. A-96-331.) In addition, section 85313(b) prohibits you from
accepting a contribution to your officeholder account totaling more than $250 per calendar year
from any one person.
2 Further, section 853 13(c) prohibits you from accepting a contribution to
your officeholder account from, through, or arranged by a registered state or local lobbyist who
engages, or is authorized to engage in lobbying the Fresno City Council.
You would like to know whether you may raise funds during the off-year ban for the
purpose of opening a district office. Expenditures from your officeholder account formed
pursuant to section 853 13(a) must be used for expenses related to assisting, serving or
communicating with your constituents, or carrying out your official duties as a member of a city
council. Using officeholder account funds to open a district office in order to assist, serve or
communicate with your constituents or to carry out your official duties is a permissible
expenditure under section 85313(a). (Bauer Advice Letter, No. A-97-040.) However, section
853 13(a) prohibits you from using officeholder account funds in connection with any campaign
for elective office or ballot measures. Therefore, ifyou establish a district office with
officeholder account funds, you may not use your district office in connection with any campaign
for elective office or ballot measures.
You have not indicated any specific expenses relating to opening a district office for
which you want to use your officeholder account funds. Please note that any office equipment
purchased with officeholder account funds may only be used to assist, serve or communicate
with your constituents or to carry out your official duties. (Section 85313(a).) Thus, such
2 Section 82047 defines “person” as an individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture,
syndicate, business trust, company corporation, limited liability company, association, committee, and any other
organization or group of persons acting in concert.


File No. A-97-062
Page 3
equipment may not be used for personal purposes.
3 Moreover, such equipment may not be used
in connection with any campaign for elective office or ballot measures.
(Id.)
You would also like to know whether you may locate the district office inside your home.
As discussed above, section 85313 establishes limitations on contributions to an elected official’s
officeholder account. These limitations, however, do not apply to an official’s contributions of
his or her personal funds to his or her officeholder account. (Sections 8530 1(e), 853 07(c) and
89511.5(a).) Accordingly, your district office maybe located in your home.
Carryover Funds
You may use campaign funds raised prior to January 1, 1997, for any purpose permitted
by sections 89510-89518, inclusive. (Emergency Regulations 18519.4, 18530.1, copies
enclosed.)
4 Such funds maybe used to open a district office. (Boger Advice Letter, No. 1-91-
291.) Such funds may also be used for a future election. However, before you use all or part of
such funds for a future election and the funds are not already in an account designated for a
future election, they must be transferred to a committee formed for that election pursuant to
sections 85200 and 85201.
(Johnson Advice Letter, No. A-96-361(a).) You may also transfer
up to $10,000 of such funds to your officeholder account opened pursuant to section 853 13(a).
(Emergency Regulation 18531.3, copy enclosed.) However, if you transfer $10,000 into your
officeholder account, you may not raise or deposit any additional contributions into that account
until the next calendar year.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
Qj,*fr-QA
By:
Julia Butcher
Graduate Assistant, Legal Division
SGC:JB:ak
Enclosures
Please note that legal title in any office equipment purchased with officeholder account funds must reside
in the name of your officeholder committee.
Emergency regulations expire by operation of law 120 days after adoption. Regulations 18519.4,
18530.1 and other emergency regulations will expire on May 7, 1997. The Commission may adopt the regulations
as permanent regulations before the expiration date.

CONCLUSION: 1) You may raise up to $10,000 per calendar year to establish and maintain an
